author: @sydur_graham
text: Cool idea, but HOLY SH*T these videos are so long and you barely even get to see the thing actually in use. People on thingiverse just want to see the thing in use, not hear about the history of gears and all your thoughts in these *10 minute* videos! It's maddening. Just show the item actually being used at the beginning of the video or upload 2 videos with these ones as the second.

author: @croustibat682
text: So many thanks for the stl generator. I used openscad previously and it was such a pain ... Note citroen was maybe the first (probably the last too) to use those herringbone gears in their gearboxes; thats were their logo comes from.

author: @curiosity2314
text: I did try making this gear set on the flsun sr and the gears are glued together. Any suggestions on settings that might help? I used Cura the latest version with the correct printer. Kind of a nub and could use some help. It's like the lash tolerances were to tight; only by a little. I also sent this message on Thingiverse Thanks in advance. Gerald

author: @JessWLStuart
text: Robert, I love your videos and exuberance!  Have you considered making a Taurozzi Pendulum Engine (it has curved cylinders to reduce friction)?  Since it doesn't have much friction, it doesn't need lubrication.  They use an air compressor variant to produce high pressure gasses without contamination from lubricants.  I wonder if it would make a better Stirling Engine?

author: @johnwynne-qx6br
text: Interesting video 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Glad you think so!

author: @nunyabidniz2868
text: At 1:00, where you illustrate a "double-helical gear," you're displaying a herringbone gear. The point to a helical gear is that you're trading greater friction in the gear train and a minor increase in the difficulty of cutting the gears for a reduction in end thrust; the herringbone gear eliminates the end thrust, but the tradeoff is a MUCH greater co$t in cutting said gear form, and alignment of mated gears is critical.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - i knew someone would say that - but what they - thanks lol

author: @stevennope8864
text: Hey Robert, I just watched a short video about The Curta Mechanical Calculator. I think it would be incredible to see it made with 3d printing, though it would far outstrip my drafting and cad design skills. I'm guessing it would have to be scaled up to work, but a large chunky expanded calculator would look awesome. I might try to dig up a patent or schematic and try to build one myself, though I'm not at all confident in my abilities to do so, lol. Still, would be fun. Would love to see what you could do with it and it fits nicely with your current series. Making a calculator using lots of gears and 3d printing. It just sounds awesome. I want to build it at a desk lamp sized scale, with the entire insides expanded to see it work, and with different colors for each connecting piece. I'm gonna end up trying it myself, I'm sure.🤣🤣

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think there is a chap called Michael Wu who dod an awesome job of that and released his files on thingiverse mate

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I'll check it out. I guess I got to excited to share, lol. Should've checked there first.
